classification.device_type: Drug-eluting coronary stent (long-term implant, combination product)

readiness.note: MVP stage with no detected QMS, technical documentation, clinical evidence, or CDSCO interaction. Class D implants typically need 24–36 months of foundational work before submission.

risk.rationale: Long-term coronary implant in a critical clinical state with drug-elution; among the highest-risk regulated device categories in India.

timeline.anchor: Class D implant via CLA (MD-7 → MD-9), with pivotal clinical investigation under MD-22 likely required even with an Indian predicate; combination-product review adds review cycles.

cdsco_mdr.rationale: Long-term coronary implant with drug-elution; Class D under MDR 2017, central authority pathway.
cdsco_mdr.pathway_note: Likely MD-7 -> MD-9 manufacturing licence via CLA; pivotal clinical investigation under MD-22 typically expected before licence grant. Combination product (device + drug) generally triggers additional drug-side review coordination with CDSCO.

cdsco_pharmacy.rationale: Not a drug-retail / wholesale / e-pharmacy operation; the drug coating is regulated within the combination-device review under MDR, not via the D&C Act pharmacy regime.

dpdp.rationale: Patient health data (PHI) is processed during clinical investigation, registry, and post-market surveillance; DPDP applies. SDF designation unlikely at MVP scale.

icmr.rationale: Pivotal clinical investigation in a Class D implant will need Ethics Committee approval under ICMR 2017 guidelines and CTRI registration.

abdm.rationale: Hospital deployment may eventually involve ABHA linkage for implant registry / post-market follow-up, but ABDM is not a launch-gating requirement for the device itself.

nabh.rationale: Coronary stents are typically procured by tertiary cardiac-care hospitals; NABH-accredited buyers increasingly require vendor documentation aligned to NABH digital and quality standards.

mci_telemed.rationale: No teleconsultation feature; the device is implanted by interventional cardiologists in a cath-lab setting.

irdai.rationale: No insurance distribution or aggregation feature; reimbursement coding is handled by hospitals and payers, not by the manufacturer.

nabl.rationale: Bench testing (mechanical, drug release, biocompatibility) typically uses NABL-accredited or equivalently-recognised labs; you don't operate a lab directly, but your test partners likely need accreditation.

top_gaps.gap_title: ISO 10993 blood-contact and implant biocompatibility evidence not documented
top_gaps.fix_action: Scope ISO 10993-4 (blood interactions), -6 (implantation), -10 (sensitisation), and -11 (systemic toxicity) testing with a recognised lab; this is foundational for any MD-7 filing on a long-term blood-path implant.

top_gaps.gap_title: No clinical evidence base for a Class D coronary implant
top_gaps.fix_action: Engage a clinical-research organisation to design a pivotal investigation protocol (MD-22), identify investigator sites, and prepare an Ethics Committee + CTRI submission package.
Engage an ISO 13485 consultant for a gap assessment this quarter; a certified QMS is effectively a precondition for MD-7 application and combination-product review.

card.verdict: Likely Class D drug-eluting implant — among the highest-risk device categories under MDR 2017. At MVP stage with no detected QMS, biocompatibility evidence, or clinical data, a realistic foundation-to-submission window is 24–36 months via CLA (MD-7/MD-9) with a pivotal clinical investigation (MD-22).
card.why_regulated: A drug-eluting coronary stent is a long-term blood-contact implant that also delivers a drug; this combination places it in the highest CDSCO class (D) under MDR 2017, with central-authority review.
card.tier0_card_tagline: Class D coronary implant · 24–36 month foundation before submission
card.tier1_teaser: ₹499 unlocks the full DMF skeleton (biocompatibility, sterilisation, stability, drug-substance dossier), MD-7/MD-9 filing checklist, and a Class D clinical investigation playbook tailored to coronary stents.
card.tier2_teaser: ₹25K unlocks a draft DMF, MD-22 clinical investigation protocol scaffold, ISO 13485 + ISO 14971 implementation plan, and a CDSCO pre-submission meeting brief.

inference_markers.label: Information role
inference_markers.basis: Inferred from device description — a stent physically treats coronary occlusion. Hardware founders don't answer Q2 directly.
inference_markers.correctable_at: editor or by changing your one-liner

inference_markers.label: Year-1 users
inference_markers.basis: Hardware launches typically stay below 10 lakh users in Year 1. Correct in your editor if you expect scale that triggers DPDP SDF designation.

inference_markers.label: Sterile device
inference_markers.basis: Long-term implants with direct blood-path contact are sterile-supplied by default.

inference_markers.label: Drug content
inference_markers.basis: Device description explicitly states 'drug-eluting' — this is a combination product, triggering CDSCO drug-side review coordination on top of the device pathway.

inference_markers.label: Ionising radiation source
inference_markers.basis: Coronary stents do not emit or contain ionising radiation. Confirm in editor if a radioactive variant applies.

inference_markers.label: Intended population
inference_markers.basis: Assumed humans-only; if your device targets veterinary use, the path changes (DAHD NOC).

inference_markers.label: Manufacturing location
inference_markers.basis: No explicit address detected; defaulted to India. Correct if you manufacture abroad and intend to import (MD-14/MD-15).

inference_markers.label: CDSCO class
inference_markers.basis: Class D — derived from long-term implant contact (>30 days) + drug-elution (combination product). Either signal alone would lift this to Class C/D; together they anchor Class D.

inference_markers.label: Measuring function
inference_markers.basis: A drug-eluting stent does not measure or display physiological parameters; it is a passive implant with controlled drug release.

inference_markers.label: Software component
inference_markers.basis: No software/firmware/AI mentioned. IEC 62304 and SaMD-side documentation not surfaced as gaps.

classification.device_type: Disposable single-use blood pressure cuff accessory (non-electrical, non-sterile, intact-skin contact)

readiness.note: Class A non-sterile non-measuring accessory — lowest-risk CDSCO tier. Foundations (QMS, DMF) needed before SLA portal registration; clinical-evidence dimension scored lightly because indirect-vital-sign accessories typically need only equivalence claims, not new studies.

risk.rationale: Regulated as a medical device accessory under CDSCO MDR 2017, but the lowest-risk class with intact-skin contact only, no sterility, no measuring function, and an Indian predicate available. Exposure is real but bounded.

timeline.anchor: Class A non-sterile non-measuring accessory; SLA portal self-notification path typically clears in 4–8 months including DMF assembly and ISO 13485 readiness.

cdsco_mdr.rationale: Accessory to a notified medical device (BP monitor); regulated as Class A under MDR 2017. Lowest-risk tier with Indian predicate available.
cdsco_mdr.pathway_note: Class A non-sterile non-measuring: SLA portal self-notification on cdscomdonline.gov.in; no MD-3/MD-5 application and no pre-grant audit. Indian predicate (Q8) means MD-26/MD-27 pre-permission likely not required.

cdsco_pharmacy.rationale: No drug sale, dispensing, or distribution.

dpdp.rationale: Physical disposable accessory with no personal data processing per Q6.

icmr.rationale: No AI/ML, no clinical validation study planned, no investigational claims.

abdm.rationale: No digital health record integration; Q5 confirms no ABDM/FHIR scope.

nabh.rationale: Sold to HCPs (Q3); NABH-accredited hospitals increasingly require NABH-compliant procurement documentation for consumables.

nabl.rationale: No own lab operations, no IVD function.

top_gaps.fix_action: Engage an ISO 13485 consultant for a gap assessment scoped to a single-product Class A line; certification is expected by CDSCO at SLA portal registration even for Class A.

top_gaps.gap_title: Device Master File (DMF) and Plant Master File (PMF) not assembled
top_gaps.fix_action: Begin DMF assembly covering device description, materials (cuff fabric, tubing), biocompatibility rationale for intact-skin contact (ISO 10993-10 sensitisation), and labelling; PMF can follow once manufacturing site is finalised.

top_gaps.gap_title: No CDSCO SLA portal account or self-notification draft
top_gaps.fix_action: Create a cdscomdonline.gov.in account this week and map the Class A self-notification checklist; for non-sterile non-measuring accessories this is the operative pathway, not MD-3.

card.verdict: Likely regulated as a Class A medical device accessory under CDSCO MDR 2017 — the lowest-risk tier with a self-notification path and an Indian predicate already available. Foundations (ISO 13485, DMF) are the immediate gaps.
card.why_regulated: A blood-pressure cuff is an accessory to a notified medical device, which brings it within MDR 2017 scope regardless of being non-electrical. Intact-skin contact, non-sterile, and non-measuring keep it at Class A — the floor of regulation, not the ceiling.
card.tier0_card_tagline: Class A accessory — lowest regulatory tier, but ISO 13485 + DMF still expected.
card.tier1_teaser: ₹499 unlocks the full DMF outline, ISO 10993-10 biocompatibility rationale template, and SLA portal self-notification checklist tailored to disposable accessories.
card.tier2_teaser: ₹25K unlocks the drafted Class A self-notification dossier, DMF/PMF skeletons, ISO 13485 scope-of-application document, and predicate-equivalence narrative ready for SLA portal upload.

inference_markers.label: Information role
inference_markers.basis: Inferred from your device description — a passive cuff accessory does not drive or diagnose. Hardware founders don't answer this directly.
inference_markers.correctable_at: editor or by changing your one-liner

inference_markers.label: Year-1 users
inference_markers.basis: Hardware launches typically stay below 10 lakh users in Year 1. Correct in your editor if you expect scale that triggers DPDP SDF designation.

inference_markers.label: Sterile device
inference_markers.basis: Stated explicitly in your one-liner: 'non-sterile'.

inference_markers.label: Drug content
inference_markers.basis: Most healthtech hardware contains no drug substance — confirm in your editor if your device is drug-eluting or drug-coated.

inference_markers.label: Ionising radiation
inference_markers.basis: Most healthtech hardware does not use X-ray, CT, or radioactive sources — confirm in your editor if yours does.

inference_markers.label: Veterinary use
inference_markers.basis: Assumed humans-only; if your device is for animals, that path uses DAHD NOC and you should correct this.

inference_markers.label: Manufacturing location
inference_markers.basis: No address detected; defaulted to India. Correct if you manufacture abroad — import path uses MD-14 instead.

inference_markers.label: Measuring function
inference_markers.basis: A passive cuff accessory does not itself measure pressure — the connected monitor does. This keeps you in the lowest Class A sub-tier (self-notification only).

inference_markers.label: Software in device
inference_markers.basis: No software, AI, or firmware language detected in your one-liner. Pure-hardware path applies; IEC 62304 and ACP scope skipped.

inference_markers.label: CDSCO class
inference_markers.basis: Derived from intact-skin contact + non-sterile + non-measuring + no drug + no radiation. This is the lowest sub-tier of Class A and uses SLA portal self-notification rather than MD-3/MD-5.

classification.device_type: Bluetooth-connected blood glucose meter (IVD) + companion app

readiness.note: MVP stage with no detected QMS, technical documentation, clinical evidence, or CDSCO interaction. Foundational work needed before submission.

risk.rationale: Class C IVD with direct blood-path contact and companion software syncing PHI to ABDM and clinics; clinical-management impact for diabetic patients.

timeline.anchor: Class C IVD via CLA (MD-7 → MD-9), with Indian predicate easing the substantial-equivalence narrative but bench + clinical performance evaluation still required.

cdsco_mdr.rationale: Self-monitoring blood glucose meter is a notified IVD; Class C central-licensing pathway likely applies.
cdsco_mdr.pathway_note: Indian international comparables exist, which generally eases the substantial-equivalence narrative; bench performance + clinical performance evaluation typically still required. Companion app inherits the device's class as SiMD.

cdsco_pharmacy.rationale: No drug sale, distribution, or dispensing.

dpdp.rationale: Processes patient health data (PHI) including glucose readings synced to ABDM and the clinic; sensitive personal data under DPDP. SDF designation depends on user scale.

icmr.rationale: No AI/ML component declared; ICMR AI guidelines do not apply, but EC-approved clinical performance evaluation for the IVD is typically required and follows ICMR ethical norms.

abdm.rationale: Explicit ABDM integration declared; HIP/HIU milestones (M1/M2/M3), FHIR R4, OAuth 2.0, and CERT-In Safe-to-Host certification typically apply.

nabh.rationale: Primary-care clinic integration may pull in NABH digital health expectations depending on customer mix; not a direct gating regulation for the device itself.

mci_telemed.rationale: No teleconsultation feature described; data sync to clinic is not equivalent to RMP-mediated remote consultation.

irdai.rationale: No insurance distribution, aggregation, or underwriting feature.

nabl.rationale: IVD performance evaluation typically requires NABL-accredited (ISO 15189) lab validation against the predicate device.

top_gaps.fix_action: Scope ISO 10993-4 (blood contact) and ISO 10993-10 (sensitisation) testing with an NABL-accredited lab; lancet and test-strip materials carry blood-path-direct contact.

top_gaps.gap_title: IVD clinical performance evaluation against Indian predicate not initiated
top_gaps.fix_action: Design a comparative performance study against your declared Indian predicate (accuracy, precision, interference) at an NABH/NABL site under EC approval; this is likely required for MD-9.
Engage an ISO 13485 consultant for a gap assessment this month; CDSCO Class C manufacturing license requires a functioning QMS at facility inspection.

card.verdict: Likely Class C IVD on the central CDSCO pathway with declared ABDM integration. Foundational gaps across QMS, biocompatibility, and clinical performance evaluation need to close before MD-7 filing. Since your product has a hardware component, state FDA approval may also apply depending on your manufacturing setup.
card.why_regulated: A self-monitoring blood glucose meter directly measures a serious clinical parameter via blood contact and drives diabetic patients' day-to-day management decisions — squarely within CDSCO MDR 2017 as a notified IVD.
card.tier0_card_tagline: Class C IVD on the central pathway — Indian predicate helps, but biocomp + clinical evaluation are the long poles.
card.tier1_teaser: ₹499 unlocks the full DMF skeleton: MD-7/MD-9 form mapping, ISO 10993 test matrix scoped to your contact tier, predicate-comparison template, and ABDM milestone sequencing.
card.tier2_teaser: ₹25K unlocks a regulator-grade Draft Pack: pre-filled MD-7, biocompatibility test plan, IVD performance-evaluation protocol, ISO 13485 gap report, and DPDP+ABDM integration brief.

inference_markers.basis: Most healthtech hardware does not use X-ray, CT, or radioactive sources — confirm in your editor if yours does.

inference_markers.basis: Class C — derived from blood-path-direct contact (IVD with patient sampling), non-drug, non-radioactive; measuring function with clinical-management impact.
